exemplar.title: To what extent is Coastal management effective at Winchelsea beach?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is Coastal management effective at Winchelsea beach?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is sharply phrased, explicitly geographical and rooted in coastalâprocess theory, facilitating clear hypothesis formation.
Geographic context is detailed and precise, situating Winchelsea Beach within Pett Level with physical and human significance explained.
Mapping is clear with three scale maps and a site sketch, all following cartographic conventions (north arrows, scale bars).
weaknesses: Hypothesis 2 lacks explicit theoretical justification linking beach profile differences to coastal processes.
Figure 2.1 is missing a scale bar, reducing spatial accuracy.
Figure 2.2 omits a north arrow; directional orientation should be added for completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three sub-strands fully meet the top-band descriptors: the question is geographical with clear theory links, the location is precisely defined with feasible data collection, and maps adhere to conventions. Minor mapping omissions are addressed in weaknesses but do not detract from a Band 3 performance.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Method matrix comprehensively lists equipment, procedures, sampling strategies with clear links to hypotheses and theory.
Procedures are thoroughly explained, including step-by-step protocols and justification of sampling design.
Data collection yielded sizeable, high-quality primary datasets (pebble sites, groyne profiles, repeated wave counts) with appropriate implementation.
weaknesses: Weather conditions are noted but not linked to impacts on wave-count reliability.
Beach-profile methodology is referenced but not detailed in the methods section.
Justification for the number of wave-count repeats is minimal; the rationale for sample size should be expanded.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigationâs methods are comprehensively explained and theoretically aligned, and data collection techniques produced reliable primary data, satisfying all Band 3 descriptors for Criterion B.

strengths: Collected variables (pebble metrics, groyne exposure, wave frequency) are directly relevant and largely sufficient for meaningful statistical analysis.
Comparative bar graphs and scatter plots effectively highlight site differences and anomalies.
T-test tables are well presented with means, standard deviations and variances clearly shown.
weaknesses: Beach-profile data, promised in methods, are missing from the results, creating a minor data gap.
Axis labels on some graphs lack units and contain inconsistent significant figures.
Wave-count pictogram is cluttered and unclear; simpler numeric tables would improve readability.
Bibliography entries vary in formatting; a consistent citation style should be adopted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Most data are relevant and sufficient for analysis, and presentation generally uses appropriate techniques. Occasional lapses in convention adherence and missing profile data prevent full Band 6 but justify a mark at the upper range of the Band 5â6 descriptor.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate statistical techniques are applied (Spearmanâs rank, two-sample t-test) with formulae and a 95 % confidence interval provided.
Trends and anomalies are interpreted in relation to hypotheses and coastal-process theory (e.g., sediment transport dynamics).
Analysis links results back to theory and methods, demonstrating a sophisticated understanding of data patterns.
weaknesses: Beach-profile analysis is absent despite being outlined in methods, leaving a gap in the evidence base.
Degrees of freedom are misquoted (df = 64) and should be corrected to reflect sample sizes.
The p-value interpretation conflates probability of the null hypothesis with data likelihood; this needs conceptual clarification.
Reported percentage change (~184.49 %) appears miscalculated and requires revision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates appropriate use of descriptive and statistical techniques and links interpretations to theory, placing it firmly in the 5â6 band. Minor computational errors and gaps in one dataset preclude a top-band score but warrant the upper mark of 6.

strengths: Conclusion is clear, concise, directly addresses the research question and references statistical outcomes.
Hypotheses are explicitly accepted or rejected based on analysis, demonstrating coherence with evidence.
weaknesses: Contradiction in the text regarding acceptance of the pebble correlation hypothesis needs reconciliation.
Summary statements could better integrate the implications of each statistical test.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion fully meets the Band 2 descriptor by providing a well-supported, clear answer to the research question, though minor internal inconsistencies should be resolved for perfection.

strengths: Strengths and weaknesses are outlined for key methods (e.g., measurement precision, subjective roundness scale).
Improvement suggestions (use of callipers, additional groyne readings, repeat days) are relevant and linked to reliability.
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks a feasibility analysis of suggested improvements (e.g., time/resource implications).
Depth of discussion on methodological redesign and potential trade-offs is limited.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation identifies appropriate strengths, weaknesses and relevant improvements, aligning with the Band 2 descriptor. However, it stops short of the comprehensive, impact-focused analysis required for Band 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Three scale maps (national, regional, site-scale) all contain north arrows and approximate scale bars; a fourth sketch pinpoints the groyne line.  Although hand-drawn, they respect basic cartographic conventions and convey spatial relationships unambiguously, satisfying the âclear locational maps following mapping conventionsâ descriptor.

The research question is sharply phrased, explicitly geographical (âTo what extent is coastal management effective at Winchelsea Beach?â) and is solidly rooted in coastalâprocess theory (longshore drift, groyne function).  Three clear hypotheses are derived from that theory, demonstrating that the question lends itself to testable expectations.  Hence, the descriptors for the top bandâgeographical focus, clear theoretical link, and facilitation of hypothesis formationâare fully met.

Scatter graphs, comparative bar charts and pictograms are suitable for the data, and anomalies are highlighted.  However, the wave count table is cluttered and some axis labels (m vs cm) are inconsistent, so techniques are âappropriateâ rather than âmost effectiveâ, justifying a mid-band score.

Implementation produced sizeable primary datasets (21 pebble sites, three full groyne profiles, repeated wave counts).  Sampling strategies (random, systematic transects, temporal repetition) are appropriate and executed with few evident flaws, yielding data that are broadly reliableâconsistent with the top-band statement that techniques âyield high-quality primary dataâ.

The student applies Spearmanâs rank and a two-sample Student t-test, provides formulae and a 95 % confidence interval.  Descriptive statistics (means, trends, anomalies) accompany the tests.  Minor computational/confidential value issues exist (df mis-quoted), so the work sits at the lower end of the 5â6 band.

Trends and anomalies are interpreted and consistently linked back to hypotheses (e.g., downdrift sediment build-up tied to groyne efficiency).  Coastal-process theory (attrition, swash/backwash) is referenced when explaining patterns, satisfying the 5â6 band descriptor of clear linkage, though not every dataset is exhaustively cross-referenced.

The student identifies Winchelsea Beach, specifies its position within Pett Level, East Sussex, and situates it relative to both neighbouring settlements and the English Channel.  The context explains physical vulnerability (low-lying shingle barrier) and human significance (near a populated village).  This precision clearly allows feasible primary data collection and matches band-3 expectations.

Strengths and weaknesses are outlined method by method (e.g., ruler precision, subjective roundness scale, weather limitations).  Explanations are relevant but not deeply developed across all stages, corresponding to the middle band (2 marks).

The investigation collects several directly relevant variables (pebble axis, roundness, groyne exposure, wave frequency) in quantities sufficient for statistical testing.  Minor gaps exist (beach profiles promised but not shown), yet overall the data enable meaningful analysis.  This aligns with the upper end of the 3â4 band.

A clear, concise conclusion directly addresses the research question and references statistical outcomes, accepting/rejecting hypotheses where appropriate.  Support from the analysis is adequate, so the top band for Criterion E is achieved.

Analysis covers pebble metrics, groyne exposure and wave energy, but promised beachâprofile data are absent and the wave dataset is poorly analysed.  These omissions leave some âgaps in evidenceâ, placing the work solidly within the 3â4 band at the upper score of 4.

Each method is fully itemised in a matrix that lists equipment, step-by-step procedures, sampling strategy and justification.  Links to individual hypotheses (pebble size, groyne exposure, wave energy) are explicit, and theoretical rationale (longshore drift, destructive waves) is woven into the explanation, fulfilling the âcomprehensively explainedâ criterion.

Most figures include titles, units and axes; maps have north arrows and scale.  Nonetheless occasional lapses (irregular significant figures, untidy pictogram) appear.  These âoccasional errorsâ fit the 3â4 mark band, meriting a score of 3.

Concrete improvements are proposed (use callipers, replicate groyne readings, repeat on multiple days) and potential impacts on reliability are briefly noted.  While helpful, the discussion lacks detailed methodological redesign or feasibility analysis, so a 2-mark placement is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The research question is sharply phrased, explicitly geographical, and rooted in coastal-process theory, facilitating clear hypothesis formation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 2 is stated without theoretical justification; include a rationale linking beach profile differences to coastal processes.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographical context is detailed, situating Winchelsea Beach precisely within Pett Level and explaining its physical vulnerability and human significance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 2.1 lacks a scale barâadding one would improve spatial accuracy and adhere to cartographic conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 2.2 does not include a north arrow; adding directional orientation will strengthen mapping conventions and readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Weather conditions are noted but not linked to their potential impact on wave-count reliability; explicitly discuss how rain, wind and tide affect your measurements.
Beachâprofile methodology is referenced in the introduction but not detailed here; include a procedure table or narrative for collecting profile data.
Anomalies at sites 2â5 are noted descriptively but not fully linked back to coastal-process theory; explicitly tie these patterns to longshore drift variation.
Spearmanâs rank table only shows three data pairs; the full dataset of 21 pairs is required for sufficiency and meaningful statistical analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The interpretation presents the p-value as the probability the null hypothesis is true; clarify that it indicates the probability of observing the data under the null hypothesis.
Critical probability value (P=0.50) is unusually low for significance; discuss why such a high Î±-level is acceptable or consider a more conventional threshold.
Comparative bar graphs of groyne exposure are effective and clearly distinguish site differences.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Discussion of variability lacks error bars or confidence intervals; include these or address measurement precision in the narrative.
The T-test dataset table is well presented with means, standard deviations and variance clearly shown.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Degrees of freedom are misquoted as df=64 despite sample sizes of 33 per group; recalculate df and correct interpretation.
The method matrix comprehensively lists equipment, procedures and sampling techniques with clear theoretical linksâexcellent presentation of field methods.
Justification for the number of wave-count repeats is minimal; explain why five repeats and two additional sessions adequately capture variability.
Figure 3.1 uses an appropriate scatter graph to display pebble roundness versus site number, with anomalies clearly indicated.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Axis labels on graphs lack units; include proper unit notation (e.g., cm, mm) to ensure clarity and convention adherence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The reported ~184.49% increase appears miscalculated or unclear; revisit the percentage change formula and clarify the reference values.
The t-test formula is included, which is excellent; annotate each symbol (Î£D, N, s) with your data summary to clarify the calculation steps.
Photograph effectively illustrates a groyne exposure anomaly, reinforcing the textual explanation of sediment burial.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The waveâcount pictogram table is cluttered and symbols are unclear; use standard numeric tables or simpler pictograms for readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The conclusion directly addresses the research question and integrates evidence from multiple testsâclear and well supported.
Conclusion claims all hypotheses are supported yet accepts the null for pebble correlation; reconcile this contradiction in your summary.
Evaluation outlines relevant strengths and weaknesses but would benefit from a feasibility analysis of suggested improvements.
Evaluation of pebble roundness measurement acknowledges bias and suggests callipersâa clear strength in reflecting on methodological limitations.
For calliper use, outline a protocol (e.g., calibration, measurement increments) to show how precision gains outweigh additional complexity.
Bibliography entries vary in formatting and style; adopt a consistent citation format (e.g., APA or MLA) and ensure uniform punctuation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
